b15a4780d2fd: ============ 0. Foundations: seeded RNG + fixed timestep (the short form of the determinism rules) ============
9c72a6235039: ============ 0b. String table (no user-facing text is hard-coded past this point) ============
be1e2897bb29:
Map registry. Each map supplies its size, a terrain painter, a resource
placer, starting positions and a routing style for AI attack waves.
?map=<id> picks one; the map button cycles through them (reloading, so the
world is rebuilt cleanly).
Mirroring is a hard rule: terrain and resources are always defined for the
left half and mirrored into the right, and the enemy town centre is derived
from the player's with mirTx() rather than typed in. A hand-typed enemy town
centre two tiles off centre once handed one side a compounding economic lead
and a near-100% win rate on a supposedly symmetric map.
e348d4773f89:
Water movement layers: deep water carries ships and blocks land units;
shallows slow land units down (they make natural chokepoints) and ground
ships; bridges carry land units at full speed and block ships. All three sit
at height 0, so the existing cliff system draws coastal cliffs for free.
Land and water are separate movement domains -- equal height alone would not
keep them apart, so domOK() is what actually separates them.
91ef27ca0efa:
The unit table is the AI's vocabulary as well as the game's: adding a unit is
adding a row, and the formation system picks it up without the AI logic ever
naming a concrete unit type.
role   = front / ranged / mobile / siege / anti-armour / support, the terms
         army composition is expressed in
thW    = threat weight: how much countering force one sighted unit calls for
repl   = upgrade chain: which unit this one supersedes, inheriting its quota
ulk    = the research that unlocks it
e8e3e568c72b:
mount:true marks stable-trained riders, which is the surface the horseshoe
research acts on -- horse archers included, since they come from the same
stable and the same cavalry line. Scouts are deliberately excluded: they are
not in the units array, cost no upkeep and follow their own table.
254270e073de:
cap = garrison capacity (in unit volume); rc/rT = repair cost basis and time.
Command centres come in three tiers: the capital and cities count as command
centres -- lose them all and the game is lost -- while cities additionally act
as backup lives, so a fallen capital is not the end. Outposts are cheap, do not
count as a life, and can be upgraded into cities. The capital can be rebuilt,
but only while you have none.
7d723f66a032:
Chevauchee targets: farms, coppices and fish traps -- the renewable resource
buildings. Deliberately not "all buildings": historically a chevauchee went
around castles and burned the fields, and mechanically a generic building bonus
would turn horse archers into siege units competing with the catapult.
1ae4f222496d:
Base regrowth for farms and coppices (food/wood per second). At 0.6 of a
villager's 2/s gathering rate, one farm is worth roughly one villager working
next to the town centre.
eb79e2338b0f:
============ Civilisations: one shared tech tree + a per-civilisation mask ============
One shared tree plus an enable/lock mask per civilisation is the whole backend:
adding a civilisation is adding a mask, which is pure data.
Fields: cat = category | tier 1/2/3 | kind: val stat, fn feature, unlock, uniq
unique unit | star = civilisation branch point (mutually exclusive) | at = the
building it is researched at | req = prerequisites, kept at most two deep.
0c2bd1a996fd:
dom = the AI's effect domain (economy / military / defence / intelligence /
naval). It is the key the scoring system files a demand under, so a new
research attached to an existing category gets a domain for free.
cf584ca0fbbc:
The civilisation table's AI columns exist so that adding a civilisation is
adding a row rather than editing code:
personality  opening bias, harassment, retreat threshold, expansion appetite,
             how early it walls
priorities   research order and the starred line it commits to (identity
             follows the actual civilisation; personality only shapes style)
counters     per-opponent composition multipliers and research queue-jumping
The "tell" column is derived automatically from the unique unit: sighting one
is what lets the opponent infer the civilisation.
cea2b22d9eab:
Per-side state: resources, research, civilisation, army groups, behaviour and
food supply, one set each. The AI is a player that follows the rules, so it
shares this structure and the same order functions (side is a parameter,
defaulting to the human player).
scripted = fed by free waves instead of production, and therefore exempt from
upkeep and starvation. The real AI runs with it false: same rules, same costs.
8573d58eadde:
Tables derived from the unit, research and civilisation tables. Adding a row
and calling rebuildAiTables() is enough for formations, civilisation tells and
unlocks to notice it -- no code changes.
770f1c401022:
Forests. Every tree is both a wood source and one point of sight blocking; two
stacked block sight completely, so a two-tile-thick stand cannot be seen
through and only the inside of a thicker one actually hides units.
By default, scattered single trees are marked for felling (villagers gather
them like any wood), while solid stands are left standing so ambush cover is
not auto-harvested. Felling a tile turns it to plain, which opens sight lines --
clearing woods to deny an ambush is an emergent tactic, not a scripted one.
562dbb364a6f:
Hiding capacity is not a number: how many units fit in a wood falls out of
their body radii not overlapping. Whoever does not fit stands outside in plain
sight. Nothing about the size of a forest leaks to the player before scouting.
684735ef967a:
============ Walkable regions ============
A region id per tile, so findPath can reject "unreachable, ever" in O(1)
instead of flood-filling the map on every call.
Land and water get separate tables, and shallows belong to both (land units
wade, ships crawl). Sharing one table would splice land and water into a single
region through every ford and make the whole thing meaningless.
Note this is not the same as the land/water flood fill used for island checks:
that one ignores blocked tiles and height, so a pocket walled off by cliffs
counts as connected there and must not be confused with reachability.
f6d095b69dc5:
Nearest tile of our own region to the target point -- not to the target's
region. Ordering units somewhere they cannot reach should walk them somewhere
legible rather than leave them standing still.
The Chebyshev ring is scanned outward, and because a ring of radius r reaches
Euclidean distance 1.415r, the scan continues past the first hit to be sure the
closest point is found. Ties go to whichever was scanned first, which keeps it
deterministic.
6418b8de88e0:
Mirroring helpers shared by every map: define the left half, mirror it to the
right at the same y. Symmetric terrain is a fairness rule, not a nicety.
fabbcbe81208:
terr is a zero-filled Uint8Array and T_PLAIN is 0, so an unpainted map is
already all plain.
093423154428:
One resource placer per map. The shared rule is the same as for terrain:
define the left half and mirror it. Wood comes from forest tiles unless a map
deliberately has none.
4d1b77d9147b:
There is only one kind of tree in the game. Discrete wood nodes become single
forest tiles carrying the same amount of wood: they block one point of sight,
turn to plain when exhausted, and -- being too sparse to form a solid stand --
are marked for felling by default, so the economy behaves as it always did.
2cfbd4467d61:
Every forest tile is a wood node, so felling one clears the tile, reopens sight
and recomputes the fog and forest regions.
The default marking follows the two-tile thickness rule: single trees and thin
lines are marked for felling by both sides (cutting them costs nobody any
tactical value), while solid stands are left alone so screens and ambush cover
survive. What is left of a stand mid-game keeps its marking; each side decides
for itself.
f5b5f59b12dd:
Villager behaviour has two tracks: a town bell for peacetime, and an
under-attack order of repair (if critical) > garrison > stance.
4dc0f3590f96:
Villager and scout production is queued per command centre rather than
globally, so a fallen capital does not stop a city from replacing losses.
Resources are spent on order and refunded on cancel, as with the barracks.
c6aa1b6b1992:
Three-stage starvation warnings and upkeep are per side; scripted sides pay
neither.
de78d9c9bc7e:
Development tooling: coordinate readouts and timestamps for reporting a unit
that misbehaves during play.
c59caec50ac1:
Omniscience shortcut, off by default: the AI scouts for its information like
any player. Army positions and composition are never handed to it at any
difficulty. When on, it is symmetric -- both sides see everything -- and exists
only so tests do not have to scout before asserting anything.
18492e6257fa:
Engagement model: one global engagement radius became a per-unit one derived
from sight and weapon range, plus group support.
Attack orders are attack-moves: each unit engages within max(sight, range) along
the way and pursues without a leash, leaving the decision to disengage to the
player. Reaching the ordered point survives the detour -- units finish the walk
after a chase, then hold there. Guard and patrol use the more conservative
min(range, cap) and stay anchored to their post.
Support works off the centroid of a group's engaged cluster: idle members
nearby join in, distant ones are not dragged across the map. That is what stops
reinforcements trickling in one at a time and stops fights daisy-chaining across
the map.
bd6f45a92631:
Cliff thickness. A top-down view cannot show height, so a cliff has to read as
width -- and the width you see is exactly the keep-out width used for
collision, which turns cutting a corner across a cliff from something the code
fights into something the geometry forbids.
A cliff edge is highland meeting plain, forest or marsh; ramps differ by too
little to count, so cliffs break naturally where a ramp is.
c772a4cfb6f3:
Building footprints in tiles. Gates are the one rectangle: 2x1 against a
horizontal wall, 1x2 against a vertical one.
7fe53f141bf6:
Completion hooks: a farm becomes a renewable food node, a coppice a renewable
wood node -- one villager each, never exhausted.
b19634a56592:
Starting positions: yours comes from the map table, the enemy's is always
derived from it by mirroring rather than typed in. The bug that motivated this
was exactly a typed-in value two tiles off, which shortened one side's opening
gathering loop and compounded into a lopsided win rate.
851992c1183e:
Both sides open with a town centre and villagers, nothing else. Barracks,
towers and castles are for the player to decide on -- and for the AI to decide
too: its strategy layer builds its own barracks and its fortification logic its
own towers, rather than starting with them for free.
13983ea9d4d0:
============ 4. Pathfinding (A* over tiles) ============
Six places ask "can something stand on this tile?", and they are meant to differ
-- placement rules are stricter than walking rules. What went wrong once was not
having six of them, it was not having written down the one standard they vary
from: gates are obstacles that are not in the blocked array (they are side-aware
instead), one of the six missed that, and an order onto an enemy gate sent A*
flood-filling the whole map before returning nothing.
77b5c98bea79: An enemy gate blocks; your own is walkable. Defined here and nowhere else.
cd96abc1b13f:
Straight-line walkability, sampled: no blocked tile, no cliff crossed between
samples. Used for path smoothing and for deciding whether a chase can go
straight or has to path around.
2a107bc86198:
Slow ground -- marsh and shallows -- may not be smoothed through. A* already
routes around it by cost, but string-pulling would happily straighten the detour
back across the marsh, which is why units appeared to ignore it. The rule only
applies when both ends of a segment are on fast ground: a path that genuinely
uses a ford has its A* nodes on the slow tiles, so fords still work.
a1bc49a2085f:
Path smoothing (string-pulling): turn A*'s staircase into long straight runs,
always keeping the final node.
ec10cfed8b16:
Pathfinding counters -- cross-region fallbacks and genuine failures. Readable
from the console, so "it cannot get there" is visible in probes and in play.
a2bdc76fbe0e:
The open set is a binary min-heap. It used to be a plain array scanned linearly
for the minimum, which is O(n^2) and was fine on a small map; at 128x72 tiles a
single call went from about 0.1ms to 2ms, and profiling put pathfinding at 85%
of all simulation time -- the sole cause of the frame-time spikes.
This is a pure data-structure swap and must not change behaviour by one bit, or
every balance baseline shifts. The old linear scan compared strictly, so ties
went to whichever was pushed first; the heap reproduces that with an insertion
sequence number as the tie-break.
285963548fc7:
Sight through trees: each forest tile on the ray blocks one point, two block it
completely. The target's tile counts, the observer's does not -- so a single
tree hides nothing, and hiding means standing behind the second row. Looking
out of a wood follows the same rule, so standing too deep blinds you too and
the best ambush position is the second row.
Concealment is what emerges from that rule; there is no "forests are special"
case anywhere.
191c561138af:
Danger avoidance. Tiles within three of a known enemy unit are marked
dangerous, and villagers' automatic pathing treats that as a soft cost -- go
around if going around is possible.
This only applies where the player has not expressed an intent: an explicit
assignment, a nudge, or the keep-working stance all ignore danger.
A second level marks the firing envelope of seen enemy defences plus a buffer.
That one is hard: villagers will not gather inside it at all, and any caught
inside flee. Towers are not units, so the unit-based danger never saw them --
which is how villagers ended up gathering next to an enemy tower until they
died.
0bc25bb7e502:
Danger memory: a tile where an enemy was seen stays dangerous for a while, and
villagers treat its resources as unavailable meanwhile. Without it they walk
straight back to the same tower the moment it leaves their sight.
The memory is cleared by re-exploring the tile and finding it empty -- so
confirming that ground is safe takes a scout -- with a time limit as a backstop
so nothing stays feared forever inside the fog. It is never drawn: it is memory,
not a permanent marker.
dd0817e4be65:
The AI's symmetric copy: threats originating from the other side, gated through
that side's vision by the same rules, so its villagers avoid your towers and
remember dangerous ground exactly as yours do.
8aa146967b6c:
== Intelligence gate (per side; the only one in the project) ==============
What a side can currently see, and what it remembers, is answered by these four
functions only. Ask them; never read the vision buffers directly.
Why the layer exists: the omniscience shortcut used to be wired only into the
branches for side 1, a leftover from when side 0 was always the human. Once the
game could run AI against AI, that shortcut meant one side quietly played with
better information than the other -- and it surfaced as a side bias nobody could
explain for weeks.
a7dbf3fc9086:
Construction sites inside enemy defensive fire are re-checked, not just checked
once. A site can be clean when it is placed and become lethal later when the
enemy builds a tower next to it; villagers then shuttle back and forth for
minutes trying to finish an eighteen-second building.
Everything else in the danger system re-evaluates every tick -- the assigned
build site was the one thing that stayed fixed until it was finished or
destroyed. When the fire goes away (the tower falls, the garrison leaves) work
resumes automatically: one rule covering both directions.
a2cea2990005:
Cancelling a construction order also withdraws the unfinished site. Leaving it
behind meant the repair scan kept trying to staff a site that the danger rules
kept refusing, while the half-built shell went on occupying one of the AI's
expansion slots -- which is how fixing a real bug once made the AI dramatically
worse.
No refund, matching the rest of the game: cancelling construction is a sunk
cost, otherwise place-cancel-place becomes free scouting.
f9b816cbc091:
Body radius: one physical circle per unit, in pixels. People are 6, heavy
infantry 7, riders 8-9, siege 12, ships 9-12 -- roughly the square root of the
relative footprints. Separation, melee contact and drawing all use it, so what
you see is what is occupied, and how many units a wood can hide follows from it
rather than from a capacity number.
4878e84c27f2:
Concealment feedback: a unit standing on a deep forest tile -- one whose eight
neighbours are all trees, so normal sight cannot reach it -- and not currently
exposed by its own fire is drawn semi-transparent, meaning "hidden". Standing at
the treeline is drawn solid.
This is purely visual and derived from terrain the player can already see, so it
cannot leak whether the enemy sees you.
ccb83b95265e:
General movement: A* over any real distance (ramps are mandatory), straight-line
over short ones, never across a cliff.
